burial.plot_notes: Originally interred Second Presbyterian burial ground on Arch Street Philadelphia (sexton's note: 'buried in his wife's grave' — M1 Aletheia Webster's). Removed to Laurel Hill 1867 when the Arch Street churchyard was cleared — 64 years after his death.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev Nathaniel Sparhawk + Elizabeth Perkins married 1720. John was youngest of their four children.

career.notes: Proprietor of the London Book Store on Second Street Philadelphia, advertised in the Pennsylvania Packet (e.g. February 24, 1772 ad: 'A GENERAL ASSORTMENT OF BOOKS' + 'AN ASSORTMENT of CURIOUS HARD-WARE'). Most notable historical role: Philadelphia publisher of Josiah Quincy Jr's 1774 pamphlet 'Observations on the Act of Parliament Commonly Called the Boston Port-Bill,' distributing through agents in New York, Annapolis, Williamsburg, and Charleston.

Sandemanian elder during Revolution; persecuted by patriot militias 1777 western CT. Arrested twice, drumhead-tried, denounced as 'a Scoundrell not fit to live in this world'; £1,200 bond; rearrested with Ebenezer White + Munson Gregory after British withdrawal; all three threatened with summary execution. Per Daniel Humphreys: 'his being a Leader among the Brethren... was what made him so obnoxious.'

notable.notable_blurb: Philadelphia bookseller, published Quincy's Boston Port-Bill 1774

narrative_blocks.body: As proprietor of the London Book Store on Second Street Philadelphia, he published the Philadelphia edition of Josiah Quincy Jr's 1774 pamphlet 'Observations on the Act of Parliament Commonly Called the Boston Port-Bill,' advertising it in the Pennsylvania Journal and distributing through agents in New York, Annapolis, Williamsburg, and Charleston. The network helped one of the major patriot pamphlets reach colonizers far beyond New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Sandemanian elder arrested twice during Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: A Sandemanian elder at Danbury CT during the Revolution. Confronted by patriot militia returning home late one night, arrested, and subjected to a drumhead trial — denounced as 'a Scoundrell not fit to live in this world' for his religious refusal to support the American cause. Released on £1,200 bond, then rearrested with Ebenezer White and Munson Gregory after the British withdrew, all three threatened with summary execution.

narrative_blocks.header: Patent medicines, pistols, pinchbeck buckles, books
narrative_blocks.body: His 1772 Pennsylvania Packet advertisement devoted more space to 'AN ASSORTMENT of CURIOUS HARD-WARE' than to Blackstone's Commentaries. He stocked spectacles, pocket and horse pistols with brass and iron barrels, pinchbeck buckles, knives and forks in wood boxes or shagreen cases. Tradition holds Thomas Jefferson stopped at the London Book Store on July 4, 1776 — the day the Continental Congress approved the Declaration — and bought ladies' gloves and a thermometer.

narrative_blocks.header: Reinterred at Laurel Hill sixty-four years later
narrative_blocks.body: Originally buried Second Presbyterian Church burying ground on Arch Street Philadelphia, per the sexton's note 'in his wife's grave' referring to his first wife Aletheia Webster. When the churchyard was cleared in 1867, his remains were removed to Laurel Hill East Cemetery Philadelphia — where his daughter Eliza and her husband Judge Joel Jones would later join him.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Smith Sparhawk (?-1838), second wife of Dr John Sparhawk X02863. Marriage record not located. Not built. M1 was Aletheia Webster (1779).

bio.title: Judge
bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT.

birth.notes: Wikipedia + biographical dictionary give Oct 26, 1795; FindAGrave gives Oct 25, 1795 — minor discrepancy. Using Wikipedia/canonical Oct 26.

marriages.notes: Per Wikipedia: 'June 14, 1831 married Eliza P. Sparhawk in Philadelphia, with whom he had six children.' Per FindAGrave 5 children named: Rev John Sparhawk Jones (TD0240 1841-1910 — built next stage); Elizabeth Jones (d.1837 young); Joel Jones (d.1837 young); Mary Jones (d.1844 young); Sarah Jones (d.1849 young). The Wikipedia '6 children' may include an unnamed sixth or a Rev John discrepancy. Only Rev John Sparhawk Jones survived to adulthood per all accounts.

education.notes: Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — INST022 in tree.

education.school_name: Apprenticeship with Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT
education.notes: Studied law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT after Yale.
Litchfield Law School 1819 under Tapping Reeve — completed legal training before being admitted to the bar in New Haven.

career.notes: Conducted an academy at Wilkes-Barre PA for a year and a half starting 1822 — after family crisis prompted his relocation from New Haven CT.
Practiced law at Easton PA for many years before relocating to Philadelphia.

Secretary to Lafayette College board of trustees 1826-1835; continued as trustee through 1852. (Lafayette College not yet built as INST — pending Kirkpatrick line stage which will add 4+ connections including grandnephew William Sebring Kirkpatrick.)

1830 appointed by Governor George Wolf as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the civil code of Pennsylvania. Published 1834 'A Syllabus of the Law of Land Office Titles in Pennsylvania.' Report submitted to PA Senate January 15, 1836.

Associate Judge Philadelphia District Court 1835-1845; Presiding Judge 1845-1847. Resigned 1847 to accept Girard College presidency. Notable Delaware River fisheries ruling upholding 1783 PA-NJ compact + private fishery rights as protected property despite federal navigation/commerce supremacy.

First president of Girard College 1847-1849. Girard's 1831 will founded the college for 'poor white male orphans' with controversial clause barring clergymen from campus (upheld by SCOTUS in Vidal v. Girard's Executors 1844).

Mayor of Philadelphia October 16, 1849 to October 15, 1850. Elected as Independent (some sources Democratic) candidate October 9, 1849 narrowly defeating Whig Charles Gilpin. Lost 1850 reelection to same Gilpin. Single-year term under pre-1854 city charter.
Elected member American Philosophical Society 1848. (APS not yet built as INST.)

Ruling elder Presbyterian Church. Theological writings: Notes on Scripture (1860 posthumous, published by widow); Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age (Girard College student use); Knowledge of One Another in the Future State; edited The Literalist (5 vols 8vo prophetic literature with original additions); translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome and of the Temporal Power of the Popes' from French. Advocate of literal Second Coming prophecy.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College B.A. 1817 with high honor.

notable.notable_blurb: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, Girard College first president

cross_connections.display_label: Federal Judge Eastern District PA; great-grandnephew via brother Matthew

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, narrow Independent victory
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Mayor of Philadelphia October 9, 1849 as the Independent (or Democratic) candidate, narrowly defeating Whig attorney Charles Gilpin to break the Whig Party's longstanding hold on the executive office. Inaugurated October 16, 1849 under the pre-1854 single-year charter. Lost his 1850 reelection bid to the same Gilpin amid the city's nativist Whig-Democrat ferment that would later fuel the Know-Nothing movement.

Resigned the presiding judgeship of Philadelphia District Court 1847 to become the first president of Girard College — Stephen Girard's 1831 endowed institution for 'poor white male orphans.' Served two years amid lingering controversy from the Supreme Court's 1844 Vidal v. Girard's Executors decision upholding Girard's will clause barring clergymen from campus. Stepped down 1849 to enter the mayoral race.

narrative_blocks.header: Commissioned to revise Pennsylvania's civil code, 1830
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed by Governor George Wolf in 1830 as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the Commonwealth's civil code. The team's report to the Pennsylvania Senate January 15, 1836 produced enduring statutory compilations.

Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — the eldest of nine Coventry CT children. Apprenticed in law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven 1817-1819 before completing at Tapping Reeve's Litchfield Law School 1819. Admitted to the New Haven bar before family crisis prompted his relocation to Pennsylvania, where he conducted a Wilkes-Barre academy 1822-1823 and then practiced law at Easton PA for over a decade.

narrative_blocks.header: Scholar of prophecy and literal Second Coming
narrative_blocks.body: Ruling elder of the Presbyterian Church, with theological writings including the posthumous 'Notes on Scripture' (1860) and 'Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age' for Girard College students. Edited five volumes of English prophetic literature as 'The Literalist' with extensive original additions, advocating literal interpretation of scriptures predicting the Second Coming of Christ — and translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome' from the French.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT (Amasa's father). Joel Sr not built as separate entry — only known through Amasa's parentage.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Founding minister of Brown Memorial, Baltimore

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family burial ground for Sparhawk-Jones / Turnbull / Kidder descendants.

cross_connections.display_label: Pastor Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia 1838-1861; theological author

narrative_blocks.header: Founding minister of Baltimore's Brown Memorial Presbyterian
narrative_blocks.body: Pastored Brown Memorial Presbyterian Church Baltimore — the Bolton Hill congregation built around the memorial endowment for George Brown, the Baltimore-Ohio Railroad co-founder. Taught history and was an early advocate for women's higher education before being called to Calvary Church Philadelphia.

narrative_blocks.header: The only child of five to survive
narrative_blocks.body: Mayor Joel Jones of Philadelphia lost four children young in the 1830s and ‘40s; this son alone survived to adulthood, and became a minister. He founded Baltimore’s Brown Memorial Presbyterian, endowed for a B&O Railroad founder.
Beyond the pulpit he taught history and argued for women’s right to it — a Presbyterian minister advocating their higher education when few did. His own daughter Elizabeth became a noted painter.
